question: 'Artificial intelligence' (AI) in business contexts most accurately refers to:
options: Robots that physically perform manufacturing tasks

Computer systems that perform tasks that typically require human intelligence — including: pattern recognition (identifying a cat in a photo), language understanding (answering a customer service question in natural language), prediction (forecasting demand based on historical patterns), and decision support (recommending next best actions); modern business AI is predominantly machine learning: algorithms that improve by learning from data without being explicitly programmed for each specific task; applied AI in business: fraud detection, recommendation engines, predictive maintenance, credit scoring, customer service chatbots, image recognition for quality control, and supply chain optimization
Any computer software used in a business
Only systems developed by major technology companies like Google and OpenAI
explanation: AI taxonomy for business: rule-based systems (if condition X then action Y) are not typically what modern business means by AI — they don't learn. Machine learning (trains on data to improve over time) is the core of modern business AI. Key ML types: supervised learning (trains on labeled examples — spam filters trained on 'spam' vs 'not spam' emails); unsupervised learning (finds patterns in data without labels — customer segmentation); reinforcement learning (learns through reward/penalty — autonomous vehicles); deep learning (neural networks enabling image recognition, NLP). The business AI reality: most 'AI' applications are narrow AI (excellent at one specific task) not general AI (human-like across domains). A fraud detection AI doesn't understand customer service; a customer service chatbot can't do fraud detection.

question: 'Robotic Process Automation' (RPA) automates which type of business tasks?
options: Physical robotic arms on manufacturing assembly lines

Repetitive, rule-based, high-volume digital tasks previously performed by humans — copying data between systems, filling out digital forms, processing invoices, reconciling financial records, generating reports; RPA software acts as a 'digital worker' that replicates the steps a human takes in software applications (clicking, typing, extracting data) without changing the underlying systems; high value for: accounts payable processing (extracting invoice data → entering into ERP → approving payment), HR onboarding (creating accounts in multiple systems for new hires), insurance claims processing (extracting data from documents → checking coverage → calculating payout)
Real-time machine learning systems that make strategic business decisions
Only manufacturing automation in factory environments
explanation: RPA economics: the ROI case for RPA is straightforward for high-volume, rule-based processes. A human processing insurance claims: handles 50 claims/day, 8 hours, with error rate of 2%. An RPA bot: handles 500 claims/day, 24 hours, with error rate of <0.1%, at ~30% of human labor cost. For a large insurer processing 10,000 claims/day: RPA reduces cost by 60–70% while improving accuracy. The RPA limitation: it's 'brittle' — the bot follows exact sequences; if the source system changes its interface (different button position, different field name), the bot fails. RPA requires ongoing maintenance when underlying systems change. The distinction from AI: RPA follows rules; ML-based automation learns from examples. Modern 'intelligent automation' combines RPA (rule-based process automation) with ML (intelligent document processing that extracts data from unstructured documents without pre-defined fields).

question: JPMorgan Chase deployed an AI system (COIN — Contract Intelligence) that analyzes legal documents. COIN processes 12,000 commercial loan agreements per year in seconds versus 360,000 hours of lawyer and loan officer time previously required. What does this represent for knowledge work automation?
options: AI can automate all legal work, making all lawyers obsolete

AI augmentation of knowledge work in defined, repetitive tasks: COIN automates one specific legal task: extracting defined data points from standardized loan agreements (interest rate type, maturity date, covenants, collateral terms); this task required lawyers to read 150-page agreements and locate specific clauses — repetitive but time-consuming; COIN does this faster, at lower cost, and with fewer errors; what COIN does NOT do: negotiate loan terms, advise on legal strategy, identify novel legal risks, argue before a court, or understand the business context of a deal; the AI augmentation insight: AI handles the data extraction (the 'cognitive drudgery'); lawyers focus on higher-value work (analysis, strategy, judgment, client relationships); this is the 'AI augments humans' pattern rather than 'AI replaces humans'
Automation of legal document processing requires no human oversight
All aspects of legal work can be automated with current AI technology
explanation: COIN and legal AI: the automation of document review is one of the first significant applications of AI in professional services. E-discovery (searching through millions of documents for relevant materials in litigation) is now largely AI-assisted. The pattern: AI handles scale + consistency + pattern recognition; humans handle judgment + context + novel situations + accountability. McKinsey research (2017): 45% of work activities can be automated with current AI technology — but only 5% of occupations can be fully automated. The critical distinction: activities within jobs vs. whole jobs. A lawyer's job has 45% automatable activities (document review, contract search, citation research) and 55% that require human judgment — AI augments the lawyer by handling the automatable 45%, allowing them to be more productive and focus on higher-value work.

question: Amazon's fulfillment centers use thousands of Kiva robots (now Amazon Robotics) to move shelves of products to human pickers, rather than having humans walk through the warehouse. What organizational and economic principle does this human-robot collaboration illustrate?
options: Robots always completely replace human workers in Amazon's fulfillment centers

Collaborative automation: optimize the combination of human and robot strengths rather than replacing humans; robots are better at: physical repetition (moving shelves thousands of times/day without fatigue), navigation (following precise paths), and consistency (same speed, same accuracy over 24 hours); humans are better at: identifying the correct item in a mixed bin, handling irregular items that don't fit robotic grippers, quality inspection (visual defects), packaging exception handling; pre-Kiva Amazon fulfillment: workers walked 15–20 miles/day in the warehouse; Kiva system: robots bring shelves to stationary human pickers; human walking is replaced with robotic shelf transport; human picking remains for the judgment task (selecting the correct item); outcome: warehouse capacity per square foot increased 50%, picking time reduced 75%, while maintaining human oversight for item selection
Automation in distribution centers always eliminates all human jobs
Warehouse robots require no human oversight or collaboration
explanation: Amazon Robotics economics: the Kiva acquisition ($775M, 2012) and subsequent deployment ($100K+ per robot) across 100+ fulfillment centers is one of the largest automation investments in history. The outcome: before Kiva, an Amazon fulfillment center with 1,000 workers handled X orders/day; after Kiva, the same space handles 2–3X orders/day with ~same workers but different work (no walking, more picking). Amazon's employment has actually grown with automation — automated picking enables lower shipping costs that increase order volume, which requires more workers. The automation-employment pattern: automation changes the nature of work more often than it eliminates it (in aggregate). The unfulfilled jobs: robotic grasping (picking items from bins without spilling or misidentifying) remains partially unsolved for irregular or fragile items — human hands are still better.

question: OpenAI's ChatGPT and Large Language Models (LLMs) can generate human-quality text, code, and analysis. A management consulting firm considers: (1) Using LLMs to automate slide deck creation; (2) Using LLMs to conduct initial research and synthesis; (3) Using LLMs to replace senior consultant judgment on strategic recommendations. Which of these applications represents appropriate vs. inappropriate AI use in professional services?
options: LLMs can replace all three tasks without any quality loss

(1) Slide deck creation: appropriate automation target — highly automatable, low judgment required (format content in standard templates), limited downside if the AI makes formatting errors that are easily caught; (2) Initial research synthesis: appropriate AI augmentation — LLMs can synthesize publicly available information, identify relevant frameworks, and draft initial analyses quickly; human review required to verify accuracy (LLMs hallucinate facts), add proprietary data, and apply context-specific judgment; (3) Replacing senior consultant judgment on strategy: inappropriate as autonomous replacement — strategic recommendations involve: client-specific context that the AI doesn't know, stakeholder dynamics requiring interpersonal judgment, organizational politics requiring cultural understanding, and accountability (the senior consultant is accountable for the recommendation; AI cannot be held accountable); these require human judgment that current LLMs cannot reliably provide
LLMs are not useful for any professional services applications
LLMs should never be used by consulting firms due to confidentiality concerns
explanation: LLM deployment in professional services: 2023–2024 has seen rapid adoption across consulting, law, finance, and medicine. The emerging governance framework: (1) LLMs for structured, high-volume, review-required tasks: document summarization, template drafting, research compilation — high automation value, human review catches errors; (2) LLMs for augmenting knowledge workers: helping lawyers draft contracts, consultants write slides, analysts build models — the human approves and is accountable; (3) LLMs as autonomous agents: decisions made without human review — currently too unreliable for consequential decisions (hallucination risk, context loss, accountability gaps). The accountability principle: LLMs cannot be held professionally accountable (no license to lose, no liability insurance, no malpractice exposure) — which means high-stakes professional decisions require a responsible human to review and own the output. This isn't anti-AI; it's a practical governance reality given current AI capabilities.

question: A retail company implements an AI algorithm to optimize employee scheduling based on: predicted customer traffic, employee availability, labor cost optimization, and regulatory requirements (minimum rest periods, predictive scheduling laws). The algorithm produces schedules that reduce labor costs by 18% but increases employee schedule variability (workers don't know their schedule more than 3 days in advance). What ethical and strategic tensions does this create?
options: Cost reduction is the only relevant factor in scheduling algorithm design

Algorithmic efficiency vs. human dignity and operational stability tensions: Cost benefit: 18% labor cost reduction on a $1B payroll = $180M/year savings — significant. Human costs: unpredictable schedules create: (1) Employee hardship (workers can't arrange childcare, second jobs, education around unpredictable schedules); (2) High turnover (workers who can't plan their lives leave); (3) Skill loss (high turnover in retail creates poor customer service); (4) Potential legal exposure (5+ US states have predictive scheduling laws requiring advance schedule notice). Strategic question: does the $180M labor savings exceed the cost of higher turnover (replacement cost + training) + reduced service quality (higher turnover = lower skill) + legal exposure + employer brand damage (negative publicity from employee advocacy)?; research shows: unstable scheduling increases retail turnover by 20–40%; at $3,000–5,000/employee replacement cost, a 1,000-person store with 20% additional turnover = $600K–$1M additional annual cost — potentially exceeding the scheduling efficiency gains
Employee scheduling preferences have no effect on business outcomes
AI-generated schedules should always be implemented without modification regardless of human impact
explanation: Scheduling algorithm ethics: The Gap and Target both experienced public controversies over algorithmic scheduling practices that maximized labor cost efficiency while creating unpredictable schedules for hourly workers. Academic research (MIT study of The Gap): implementing predictable schedules (just knowing your schedule 2 weeks in advance) increased sales by 7% — workers who could plan their lives were more engaged and gave better customer service. The stakeholder analysis: the algorithm optimized for one stakeholder (company cost reduction) while creating harm for another (employee wellbeing). The balanced algorithm design: constrain the optimization to include: minimum schedule advance notice, guaranteed minimum hours per week, limited availability changes. These constraints reduce the cost optimization but produce a system that's economically sustainable (lower turnover, better service quality, legal compliance) and ethically acceptable.

question: Uber's 'surge pricing' algorithm automatically raises prices when demand exceeds supply (e.g., New Year's Eve, severe weather, concerts). The algorithm increases driver supply and reduces demand to balance the market. Critics argue it price-gouges customers during emergencies; defenders argue it optimizes allocation. What does this case reveal about algorithmic business decision-making?
options: Dynamic pricing algorithms are always ethically justified because they improve market efficiency

Algorithmic transparency, override mechanisms, and context sensitivity: the surge pricing algorithm works as intended (economic theory supports: price increases attract more supply, demand is reduced among the least price-sensitive users, market clears efficiently) — in normal commercial contexts (concert ending, New Year's Eve). The ethical failure context: when there is a natural disaster (hurricane), a terrorist attack, or medical emergency, surge pricing feels exploitative rather than efficient — the customers facing the highest prices are often those in greatest need with the fewest alternatives. The governance question: should an algorithm override be triggered for specific contexts (declared emergencies, natural disasters) that the original algorithm design didn't account for? Uber has made contextual adjustments: price caps in declared disaster areas. The broader algorithmic governance lesson: (1) Define the contexts in which the algorithm should NOT operate as designed; (2) Build human oversight mechanisms for contextually inappropriate algorithmic outputs; (3) Communicate algorithm logic to stakeholders (transparency)
Surge pricing should never be used in any circumstances
Algorithmic decision-making always produces ethically optimal outcomes
explanation: Algorithmic governance in dynamic pricing: Amazon's price change algorithm (which changes prices millions of times per day based on competitor prices, demand, and inventory) attracted controversy when it raised prices on essential items during COVID-19 by thousands of percent. The algorithms weren't designed to distinguish 'price in normal market' from 'price during a public health emergency where customers have no alternatives.' Amazon added emergency pricing controls after significant public and regulatory backlash. The governance framework for AI business algorithms: (1) Define acceptable use contexts and excluded use contexts; (2) Set human review triggers for unusual outputs; (3) Build audit trails showing why specific decisions were made; (4) Create feedback mechanisms to detect when the algorithm produces unintended or harmful outputs. Dynamic pricing is legally and ethically accepted in most commercial contexts; it raises serious ethical problems in emergency conditions where normal market alternatives don't exist.
